paragraphs.text: She lived, isolated, for eighteen years on San Nicolas—off our Southern California shores.
When they rescued her she withered and died in the discomfort of our cramped civilization[.]

THE original of Daniel Defoe's Robinson Crusoe was, as everyone knows, a sailor named Alexander Selkirk, who, from 1704 to 1709, was marooned on the island of Juan Fernandez, 300 miles west of Chile, and from this bare record the author made his masterpiece of ingenuity, reflection and adventure—a shrine destined to live as long as youthfulness exists in the world; but few are aware that a century and a half later, another forlorn creature dwelt alone on an uninhabited island, off our California coast. And to make matters worse, this time it was a female Crusoe. Against Selkirk's five years' isolation, this poor thing lived in solitude for eighteen, without even a man Friday for company, and she probably would have ended her life on this rugged crumb of land in the Pacific Ocean, unknown and unsung, had it not been for a chance discovery of her presence there.

It was on a sunny day in July, 1853, that a party of otter men from Santa Barbara anchored their schooner off the northeastern coast of the Island of San Nicolas, some eighty or ninety miles distant, and established a camp on shore. The party consisted of Capt. Nidiver, a man named Brown, an Irish cook and a crew of mission Indians. The evening after their arrival the captain and Brown strolled a mile or so down the beach, enjoying their pipes and making plans for their work. It was a moonlight [moonlit] night, almost as bright as day, with the stars shining large and clear from a low sky. As they were about to retrace their steps toward the camp Nidiver stopped suddenly, looked hurriedly around, and then knelt on his knee to examine the ground. Plainly outlined on the shore was the print of a slender, naked foot!

"My God!" the captain exclaimed. "There is a woman on the island!" At dawn the next day he and Brown returned to the spot and found a basket of rushes hanging from a tree. It contained bone needles, thread made of sinews, shell fish-hooks, ornaments and a partly completed robe of birds' plumage, the feathers pointing downward, and so skillfully matched as to seem one continuous sheen of changeful luster. Nidiver proposed leaving the things untouched, but Brown scattered them about, saying that if they were picked up it would be proof the owner was alive and had visited the place. Extending their search inland, they discovered several circular, roofless inclosures, made of woven brush. Near these shelters were poles, with dried meat hanging from elevated cross-pieces. In the fissures of the rocks, near a spring of fresh water, were wedged dried fish and seal blubber; but there was no sign of the near presence of the one to whom the provisions belonged.

After several days' search the men abandoned the task and started hunting the otters. This lasted for weeks and the well-loaded schooner was preparing for the return trip to Santa Barbara when Capt. Nidiver proposed further search for the woman.
"If she is alive we ought to find her," he said. "If dead, we will look for her body even if we have to scrape the island inch by inch."

The captain ordered preparations made and the entire crew started on what the Irish cook called a woman-ghost hunt. Near the head of the island they came across a bone house, skillfully interlaced with rushes. An olla and stove-in basket were near the door, but all around the weeds were untrampled and the whole place had a deserted look. Still Nidiver urged his companions on. After several days' march, a dangerous climb over slippery rocks brought them to a spot where there were fresh footprints made by the same slender naked foot. These were followed up the cliffs until they were lost in the thick moss that covered the ground. Brown, walking on ahead, sighted a moving object long way off on the hills. At first glance it appeared like a bird, but it moved about in such a singular manner that he advanced stealthily to find out what it really was. Drawing nearer, he was dumfounded [dumbfounded] to discover it was the head of a woman, barely visible above the low woven-brush sides of her roofless retreat in the bushes.

As the sailor approached nearer, a pack of tamed wild dogs, crouching at the woman's feet, began to growl; but without looking around she uttered a peculiar cry which immediately silenced them, and they ran away to the hills. Brown halted within a few yards of her and, himself unseen, watched every movement within the hut. Inside the inclosure was a mound of grass, woven baskets filled with various articles and a rude knife made of a piece of iron hoop, thrust into a wooden handle. A fire smoldered near and a pile of bones lay in the ashes.

The complexion of the woman, he noted, was much fairer than the ordinary Indian, her personal appearance pleasing, features regular, her hair thick and brown, falling about her shoulders in a tangled mat.

From the time he arrived within hearing, she kept up a continual talking to herself. She was leaning forward, shading her eyes with her hand, watching the men from the schooner crossing the flat land far below her dwelling. After looking at them for a while with marked anxiety on her face, she crouched in terror, but immediately started up as if to run. The men on the flat had not seen her, and Brown, putting his hat on the ramrod of his gun, lifted and lowered it to attract their attention, and when he had done so, intimated with signs that the woman was found, and that they should spread out in order to catch her if she tried to escape.

Before the men reached the cliff, Brown stepped around in sight and spoke. She gave a frightened look, ran a few steps, but instantly controlling herself, stood still and addressed him in an unknown tongue. She seemed to be between 40 and 50 years of age, in fine physical condition, erect, with well-formed neck and [ar]ms and unwrinkled face. She was dressed in a tunic-shaped garment, made of the satiny plumage of the green cormorant, low in the neck, sleeveless, and reaching to the ankle. The dress was similar to the one found in the tree. As the men came up, she greeted them each in the way she had met Brown, and with a simple dignity, not without its effect on both Indians and white men, made them welcome and set about preparing [f]ood for them from her scanty store.

There were several dialects spoken by the Indian crew, but none of them was able to converse with the strange woman, or understand a word she uttered, and they were obliged to let her know by signs that they were going to take her with them. Brown went through the motion of packing her things in baskets, shouldering them and walking in the direction of the beach. She understood instantly and made preparations to go with them. One of the heaviest loaded baskets she swung without any unusual effort over her back, and, taking a burning brand from the fire, followed after the men who were carrying her other possessions. Upon reaching the boat, she entered without hesitation, going forward to the bow, kneeling and holding to either side.

When the schooner was reached, she went aboard without any trouble and sat down near the stove in the cabin and quietly watched the men at their work. To replace her feather dress, which was subsequently sent to Rome and placed in the Vatican Museum, Brown made her a petticoat of ticking; and with a man's cotton shirt and gay handkerchief, her semi-civilized dress was completed. While Brown was sewing, she watched him closely and laughed at his manner of using a needle. She showed him that her way was to puncture the cloth with her bone needle and then put the thread through the holes. She signified that she wished to try a threaded needle and Brown gave her sewing materials, but she could not thread the needle.
Brown did it for her and gave her an old coat of Capt. Nidiver's to mend, and while she took her first sewing lesson she told by signs portions of her life on the island.

From time to time she had seen ships pass, but none came to take her off. She watched as long as she could see them, and, after they were out of sight, she threw herself on the ground and cried, but after a time she walked over the island until she forgot about it and could smile again. She had seen people on the beach several times. But she was afraid and hid until they had gone and then wept because she had not made herself known. She intimated that Brown had taken her by surprise and she would not run because she liked his face and felt he had been sent to take her to her people, who had gone away on a ship with white men. The sailor understood by her signs that at one time she had a nursing baby, which she represented by sucking her finger and placing her arm in the position of holding an infant at the breast. She waved her hand over the sea to indicate the ship sailed away, and then that she could not find her child and wept until she was very ill and lay prostrate for days in a bed of plants. She had nothing to eat but leaves. When she somewhat revived, she crawled to a spring and after a time, as her strength returned, she made a fire by rubbing a pointed stick she showed him along the groove of a flat stick until a spark was struck.

Who was this mysterious woman? This was the question that Brown asked himself, and one that naturally arises in our mind. In order to answer it in part, at least, it will be necessary to begin quite far back in history. The Santa Barbara Islands, on one of which this strangest of strange romances was enacted, lie to the southward of the Santa Barbara Channel, the nearest of the group being some twenty-five miles distant from the mainland. The islands are named Anacapa, Santa Rosa, San Miguel, Santa Cruz, Santa Catalina, Santa Barbara and San Nicolas. In the year 1542, Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo, the credited discoverer of California, landed on what is now known as San Miguel and christened it Isla de Posesion. He died on the island the following year and is buried in its sands, although his grave has never been located. But delving still further into our research we find that even berore Cabrillo's time the mainland and islands were inhabited by a populous people, the origin of whom, however, is unknown.

San Nicolas, on which the scenes of this wild romance are laid, was the outermost of the group. It was at one time the abode of a people differing in customs and modes of life from those on the mainland and neighboring islands.

A scientist, named De Cessac, engaged in collecting archeological specimens for the French government, left a well authenticated record that the relics found on San Nicolas were more elaborate and showed a superior workmanship to those he examined on the other islands and the mainland.

He also found on San Nicolas articles of warfare and domestic use which evidently belonged to a northern tribe and were similar to those found by him on the coast of Alaska. Hence he draws the inference that the island was at one time the dwelling place of the north country tribes.

It is also recorded that a ship from Boston, commanded by a Capt. Whitmore, brought down from Sitka, Alaska, a company of Kodiaks for the purpose of otter hunting on San Nicolas. These were left as permanent settlers upon the island and years of feud resulted in a massacre, in which every male islander was killed by the powerful and well-armed Kodiaks. The women were taken by the victors, lived with them and bore children.
As the years passed San Nicolas faded away strangely and rapidly, so that in 1830 less than twoscore men, women and children remained.

It was not until 1835 that the schooner Peor es Nada, commanded by Capt. Williams, collector of the port of San Pedro, was sent by the church fathers to fetch the benighted people of San Nicolas and place them under their spiritual guidance. On the way over a severe storm suddenly overtook the ship and a landing on the island was made with extreme difficulty. To make matters worse the wind continued to increase in fury. No time was to be wasted. The islanders, some twenty in number, were hurried in boats to the schooner and she immediately set sail. In the excitement and confusion it was not realized that an infant had been left behind on the island, but not many moments passed before the frantic mother made that fact known to the captain and with gestures implored him to return. But on account of the storm and the fear of wrecking his vessel he dared not comply with her request.

Finding it was useless and the ship was heading out to sea, the mother became desperate and in spite of all attempt[s] to detain her, threw herself into the water and started to swim toward the shore.
It was impossible to attempt to save her and, as she soon vanished from sight in the terrific surf, she was supposed to have perished.

After a rough passage, the schooner finally reached San Pedro, where the islanders were sent to San Gabriel Mission, fifteen miles away. It was the intention of Capt. Williams to return immediately to San Nicolas as soon as the storm abated, to see if the woman or child were living, but the schooner received orders from the owners to proceed at once to Santa Barbara and thence to San Francisco. She never reached the latter port, but was wrecked at the entrance to the Golden Gate.

Fifteen years passed and the uncertain fate of the woman and child was forgotten apparently by everyone but the tender-hearted mission fathers. Vessels were scarce, but in 1850 they managed to persuade one Thomas Jeffries, who had come into the possession of a small schooner, to visit the island and search for them. On his return he reported no sign of human life there. But evidently the woman did not perish, although her infant did, and on this sea-girt isle she lived a solitary life for eighteen years.

On arriving at Santa Barbara, Capt. Nidiver placed the woman in the care of his wife. The news of her arrival and her strange life spread rapidly, and the house was crowded with visitors. When the ship Fremont came into port soon after, the captain offered Nidiver half of all he would make if he would allow him to exhibit the woman in San Francisco. The offer was coldly refused. Nidiver said that neither he nor his wife would for a moment consider that the friendless creature should be made a show of for the curious.
The strange woman was bitterly disappointed when none of her kin could be found.

She was patient and cheerful, however, looking eagerly into every new face for recognition, and sometimes singing softly to herself. Soon she began to droop under civilization, probably missing the outdoor life of her island camp.

After a time she became too weak to walk and was carried to the porch, where she dozed for hours in the sunshine. Believing that a return to the old diet might help her, Mrs. Nidiver procured seal meat and roasted it in ashes, but when it was presented to her she could not eat the food. One day she fell from her chair and immediately lapsed into unconsciousness. It was soon evident that she was reaching the end of her life. Father Sanchez, a Catholic priest, was summoned and, after christening the nameless creature "Juana Marie," he administered the last rites of the church to the dying woman.

Within the walled cemetery at the old Santa Barbara Mission, under the shelter of its tower, the heroine of San Nicolas was interred and, to this day; should you ask, the Franciscan brothers will point out her grave.
